In multi-distribution learning paradigms, the goal is to learn a model that performs well on any distribution in a pre-defined set of interest.
In these settings, the distributions may represent heterogeneous populations of different protected or socio-economic attributes.
In robustness applications, the distributions may capture a learner's uncertainty regarding the true underlying task.
In muti-agent collaborative or federated applications, the distributions may represent agent-specific learning tasks.
The performance and optimality of a model is measured by its worst test-time performance on a distribution in the set.
The sample complexity of multi-distribution learning differs from that of learning a single distribution in several ways.

Optimal multi-distribution learning algorithms should sample on demand, taking additional samples whenever they need them and from whichever distribution they want them.
On-demand sampling is especially appropriate when some population data is scarce.
